english: parition, bźúgs-par źal-gzigs-śiṅ (bzhugs par zhal gzigs shing) appearing in a sitting posture Mil. nt. (cf. spyan-rás (spyan ras)). — źal-bsró (zhal bsro) Tar. 76, 12, Schf.: the act of consecrating, e.g. a temple.

I. sbst., also źal (zhal), źá-la (zha la), źál-rtsa (zhal rtsa) or -rdza (-rdza) Sch. clay, lime-floor, Lex.: źál-ba (zhal ba) = skyáṅ-nul (skyang nul); mťil-gyi źá-la (mthil gyi zha la) Glr. clay, cement of a floor, cf. ăr-ga (ar ga); plastering, rough-cast, sgó-la źal bgyis-te (sgo la zhal bgyis te) plastering the door with clay Glr., also applied to the anointing of sacrificial objects with butter Mil. — II. vb. to serve up food, to spread a repast Sch.
chaff and other impurities removed from the grain by washing.

Ssk. शम, to become quiet, calm, to abate, to subside; to settle, of a swelling W.; to be allayed, of passion, malice etc. Glr.; to be appeased, relieved, to cease, of pain, quarrels, intoxication, maladies etc. Glr. and elsewh.; to be atoned, blotted out, of sins Tar.; źi-bar gyúr-ba (zhi bar gyur ba) B., *źi čá-če* (zhi ca ce) W., id.; *ra, śro źi soṅ* (ra, shro zhi song) W. the drunken fit, the paroxysm of passion has passed over; źi-la soṅ (zhi la song) (the hobgoblins) became quiet, held their peace Mil.; źi-bar byéd-pa (zhi bar byed pa) to still, sooth, appease, mitigate, *źi čúg-če* (zhi cug ce) W.; źi-byed (zhi byed) a composing draught, संशमन Wise 130; more particularly with reference to the affections: to be dispassionate, not subject to any mental emotion, źi-ba čén-por gyur (zhi ba chen por gyur) he is getting very free from passion Do., v. below źi-gnás (zhi gnas); also sbst. tranquillity, calmness, and adj. tranquil, calm, źi-ba daṅ bde-légs-su gyúr-bar mdzád-du gsol (zhi ba dang bde legs su gyur bar mdzad du gsol) permit us to attain to peace and happiness Dom.; źi-bai ťabs-kyis (zhi bai thabs kyis) amicably, in a fair way Glr.; so also źi-bai gtam smrá-ba (zhi bai gtam smra ba) Glr.; źi-bas mi ťul drág-pos dul dgos dug (zhi bas mi thul drag pos dul dgos dug) Pth. if he will not submit by fair means, he must be converted or subdued by force; źi-bai źal (zhi bai zhal) Pth. the expression of calmness about his mouth, his peaceful countenance; źi-bar gśégs-pa (zhi bar gshegs pa) to go to rest, to die Cs.; źi-bai (zhi bai) or loṅs-skui lha-tsogs źe-gñis (longs skui lha tshogs zhe gnyis) Thgr. the good, the peaceable deities, opp. to those called kró-bo (khro bo); differently again the word is used in: źi-ba daṅ kró-ba daṅ źi-ma-kro (zhi ba dang khro ba dang zhi ma khro) Pth., which has been explained by Sch. as: the medium between calmness and passion, 'calm indignation'. (Cs. moreover mentions źi-ba (zhi ba) or rtag-źi-ba (rtag zhi ba), as 'a name or epithet of Iswara and certain Buddhas', so that źi-ba (zhi ba) would be equal in sound as well as in meaning to शिव, źi-ba-pa (zhi ba pa) and -ma (-ma) being his male and female disciples. A good deal of obscurity attaches, further, to the frequent mention of the źi-rgyas-dbaṅ-drag (zhi rgyas dbang drag), as the characteristic properties of the four parts of the world (v. glíṅ (gling)), and likewise as qualities and functions of the Buddhas, gods and saints, viz. allaying diseases, conferring happiness and wealth, ruling over all creatures and subduing all that is unruly and hostile; to which are to be added four kinds of burnt-offerings, in the same fourfold sense; v. Schl. 250. Finally, in mysticism the term źi-ba (zhi ba) acts a prominent part: źi(-bar) gnas(-pa) (zhi(-bar) gnas(-pa)) and lhag(-par) mtoṅ(-ba) (lhag(-par) mthong(-ba)), शमथ and विपश्यन, shortened źi-lhág (zhi lhag), implies an absolute inexcitability of mind, and a deadening of it against any impressions from without, combined with an absorption in the idea of Buddha, or which in the end amounts to the same thing, in the idea of emptiness and nothingness. This is the aim to which the contemplating Buddhist aspires, when, placing an image of Buddha, as rten (rten), (v. rten 1 (rten 1)) before him, he looks at it immovably, until every other thought is lost, and no sensual impressions from the outer world any longer reach or affect his mind. By continued practice he acquires the ability of putting himself, also without rten (rten), merely by his own effort, into this state of perfect apathy, and of attaining afterwards even to dṅos-grub (dngos grub), the supernatural powers of a saint. The stories that are related of such achievements, and with which the work of Taranātha abounds, are, notwithstanding their absurdity, readily believed by every faithful Buddhist. That there are also cases of failures, cf. smyón-pa (smyon pa).
sieve, of cane or wood Ts.
